# Chapter 01 - Chapter 1

## Draft Paragraphs (keyed)

[[CH01-P0010]] Marnette's pulse tripped over itself as the halt came, and her hand closed on the door handle before the announcement had finished. The need to move pressed up behind her ribs, too sharp to be patient. Red dust printed under her boots on the grey mat, a fine, stubborn powder that worked into every seam of the colony no matter how sophisticated the filtration. She brushed at it out of habit, the gesture small and pointless, and felt the grit smear rather than lift. It would be there tomorrow, and the day after. It would creep over everything in time, a quiet insistence that even sealed inside their habitats they were not safe, only contained, still living on borrowed minutes in a world that had never agreed to hold them. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0020]] "Residence 7B, Master Frost's quarters," the transport's voice said, calm and genderless. "Caregiver Marnette Croft, please proceed to the main entrance." The title landed like a collar. She swallowed, tasting dust and recirculated air, and fixed her grip on the handle until her knuckles ached. Proceed. As if she had a choice. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0030]] Marnette smoothed her grey tunic and checked the prayer beads were tucked beneath the collar. Their polished weight warmed against her skin, familiar as a pulse, and it steadied her more than the filtered air ever could. She had worn them since her Coming of Age, sixteen years ago now, long enough for the ritual to have worked its way into her nerves. Elom, guide my steps. The words rose without effort, as if her body said them for her. Elom, guide my hands to serve. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0040]] Forty-seven minutes in the transit tubes had been long enough for Marnette to memorise every breath she took and still arrive with her stomach tight. She had never been assigned to the Master sector. Her work belonged to the salve districts and the neutral corridors between, the medical centres, the elder wards, the hospice wings where salves were sent to fade out of sight. A Master, though. A Master dying. The idea sat wrong in her mind, too large to hold. Masters endured for eight centuries, sometimes longer. When one finally went, it shifted the colony like a pressure change, with mourning protocols, records, and lessons that outlived anyone expected to read them. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0050]] She had been chosen as Timon Frost's caregiver. Requested, the notice said, as if a word could make it ordinary. It had arrived three days ago with a messenger in a Master's household uniform, crisp enough to make her feel unclean in her own corridor. Marnette had read the paper twice, then a third time, waiting for the line that proved it belonged to someone else. She was competent. Eight years of service had trained her hands in comfort care, in pain protocols, in the quiet work of helping a body let go with some scrap of dignity. She was also still a salve, still twenty-six, with perhaps thirty-four years left to spend. Masters usually selected older caregivers for final posts, people seasoned by decades and less likely to flinch at the centre of power. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0060]] The notice had been real. Now she stood with her heart racing, palms damp despite the regulated air, as if her body refused to accept the calm the colony manufactured. She drew a slow breath and tried to find her centre in it, the way she had been taught. Duty first. Calling second. Elom had placed her here, and she could not afford to falter. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0070]] The entrance to Residence 7B opened before she could lift a hand to request it. Beyond lay a corridor that stole the air from her lungs, not for its size, although Masters never did anything small, but for its light. Sunlight, thinned and filtered through the dome’s polarised glass, fell in long bands across polished basalt tiles and turned every surface honest. In the salve quarters, light was rationed into a steady, colourless glow that made time feel unreal. Here it had weight and warmth. It even made the red dust on her boots catch and glitter, as if the colony could afford to make grit look beautiful. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0080]] The corridor looked curated rather than built. Polished basalt ran underfoot, threaded with inlaid copper and gold-toned alloy that caught the sunlight in thin, deliberate lines. The walls were hung with tapestries showing secular scenes from the colony’s earliest days: the founder stepping onto red ground, the first habitat ribs rising, agricultural domes swelling into life. It was history, but not the sacred history she had been taught to recite. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0090]] A woman waited beside an ornate door. Not a Master. Her clothes were too plain and her stance carried the careful deference Marnette knew in her bones. Another salve, older, with silver threaded through dark hair and fine lines at the eyes that spoke of decades spent watching and enduring. She wore the same grey tunic as Marnette, only cut from a finer weave, and a small silver pin fastened at her collar. Household manager. Status, granted and visible. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0100]] "You must be Marnette," the woman said, her voice soft and kept close, as if sound itself might be overheard. "Elara. Master Frost’s household manager." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0110]] "Elara," Marnette said, dipping her head the way she had been trained to. "It’s an honour to be chosen for this assignment." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0120]] Elara’s expression held steady. "An honour, yes. And a challenge." She inclined her head towards the door. "Master Frost is awake. He’s been expecting you." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0130]] Marnette followed, her steps muted on polished basalt. The scuff of her worn boots against the pristine floor tugged at her attention, but Elara gave no sign she saw it. Service taught you what to register and what to let pass. Marnette had learned to catalogue what mattered for the work and blur the rest before it could sour into questions about her place. The wealth here could have swallowed her whole, so she narrowed her focus. Door to Master Frost’s chambers. Corridor turns. Emergency exits. The inlaid metal, the tapestries, the light Masters lived inside were only backdrop, if she made them so. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0140]] "Has he been difficult?" Marnette asked, keeping her voice low. "The briefing said his condition is worsening." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0150]] "Difficult isn’t the word I’d choose." Elara stopped at the door with her hand poised over the panel, as if even this threshold required care. "Master Frost is thoughtful. Even now. Perhaps especially now." She softened her voice further. "He asked for you. Specifically. He said he’d heard good things about your work with Elder Maris last year." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0160]] Warmth spread through Marnette’s chest, quick and treacherous. Elder Maris had been difficult in the only way the dying could be, jagged with pain and fear, then sudden stillness. She had been one of the oldest salves in the colony, fifty-eight when her cells finally began to fail beyond repair. Marnette had stayed with her for six months, managing the pain, weathering the confusion, catching the brief, lucid windows when Maris spoke of her youth and the early days when everything had been rougher and rules less polished. Those stories had travelled. In the wards, in the staff corridors, a good death was noticed, even if the people who arranged the world pretended not to look. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0170]] "I heard about Elder Maris’s passing," Elara said, and for the first time her voice softened into something like respect. "She was well regarded. Many attended her service." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0180]] "She was at peace," Marnette said. "By the end, she was ready. She’d lived a full life." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0190]] "Fifty-eight years." Elara nodded once. "A good long life. For a salve." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0200]] The phrase lingered between them, sharp despite its calm delivery. A good long life. For a salve. It was meant as reassurance, and it landed like a measurement. Marnette had heard it all her life without looking straight at what it contained. Elom had shaped them for purpose and for span, that was what she had been taught, years portioned out like rations. Fifty or sixty should be enough to serve, to contribute, to find meaning in the place assigned. It was not called a limit. It was called design. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0210]] "Master Frost has lived seven hundred and ninety-three years," Elara said, her tone settling back into professional neutrality. "Seven hundred and ninety-three." The number refused to fit inside Marnette’s mind. It reached past the colony’s oldest corridors, past the stories she had been fed as scripture, into a time that felt less like history than weather. He had been there for the beginning, close enough to it that the founder’s shadow would not have been myth but a living presence. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0220]] Marnette could not make herself picture it. Seven hundred and ninety-three years was not a life, it was an era. To keep breathing through centuries, to watch faces replace faces until names became patterns, to outlast everyone you had ever touched, it sounded less like a blessing than a sentence. Yet Masters were made for it. Different bodies, different minds, built to endure what would break a salve long before the first century turned. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0230]] "Your duty, and more." Elara’s fingers moved over the panel with practised ease, and the door opened without a sound. "He has little time left. The physicians say weeks. A month, if fortune is kind." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0240]] The doorway gave onto a space that made Marnette hesitate, breath caught high in her chest. It was not a room so much as a private level, a whole slice of habitat set aside for one life. The ceiling rose into genuine height, and sunlight from vast windows fell in angles that made proper shadows, not the even, flattening glow she knew. The furnishings looked impossibly terrestrial. Dark wood with a grain that no Martian printer could fake, fabrics in colours never permitted on salve backs, art chosen for the self rather than for instruction. Everything here said individual, and her body did not quite know how to stand in it. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0250]] What undid her most was the view. The windows ran floor to ceiling, opening onto Mars itself, not merely the dome’s managed sky and gardened air. Beyond the colony’s barriers the land lay bare, mountains knifing up against the thin atmosphere, craters bruising the flats, red distance falling away to the horizon. Far out, other habitats caught the light, small bright blisters of metal and Marscrete against the rust-coloured waste, linked by transit tubes that looked delicate from here, a thin anatomy laid over a world that did not care if it lived or died. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0260]] At the centre of all that space and light stood a bed. In it lay a man reduced to essentials, skin thin as paper, bones drawn sharp beneath it, as if the years had filed him down to something almost weightless. For a moment Marnette thought of the dust beyond the dome, the way it settled and endured, and she hated that her mind reached for that comparison. He looked fragile, impossibly old, and still he held the room as if it belonged to him by right. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0270]] Timon Frost. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0280]] He did not look like a dying man. Not at first glance. He looked old, yes, his face scored deep, skin drawn thin over bone, hair worn down to a pale hush against the pillow. Then his gaze found her and the room narrowed. Clear. Alert. Unforgiving. He lay propped against pillows that held their shape too perfectly, covered in fabric that caught the sunlight with a subtle sheen. Around the bed, medical equipment formed a quiet ring of authority. Monitors glowed with vital signs, IV lines hung with measured fluids, a ventilator waited in mute readiness, and other devices sat sleek and immaculate, their purpose not immediately obvious but their cost written into every seamless edge. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0290]] The suite was organised with the same deliberate order as a clinic, only softened into luxury. Frost’s bed anchored the space, but one side held a compact office, desk and stacked data screens, all currently dark as if the room were holding its breath. The other side offered a seating area of deep chairs and a low table set for conversation, the kind she was not meant to overhear. Along the far wall stood a bookshelf, not storage for slates or projections but rows of physical books, paper bound and worn at the edges by real hands. Marnette had seen books in the archives, behind glass and rules. She had never seen so many kept in someone’s private air. Here they were not just comfort. They were declaration. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0300]] Still, it was the man in the bed who took her attention and would not release it. Timon Frost. She knew the name the way you knew a prayer, repeated until it became part of the colony’s air. One of the oldest Masters, descended from the first families, a historian who had spent centuries turning the past into authority. In person, the legend felt like a poor fit. This Frost was reduced, his body shrunken under the blankets, skin so thin the veins showed through, hands laid on the coverlet with a lightness that made her think of things that crumbled when touched. The books spoke of height and presence, a man who could still a room with a look. The look was still there. The rest of him had begun to leave. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0310]] Yet his eyes refused every other sign of decline. They were the eyes that had once ruled rooms, unsettled younger Masters, watched centuries unspool and learned where to look for the hinge of a moment. Pale blue, almost without colour, they held an intensity that made Marnette want to straighten and shrink at the same time. His gaze took her in with a single, clinical sweep, weighing her uniform, her posture, the steadiness of her breathing. It felt less like being seen than being read. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0320]] "Master Frost," Marnette said, folding into a deep bow. "I’m Marnette Croft. Elara said you were expecting me." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0330]] "Marnette Croft," he repeated. His voice was dry, a thin rasp, yet every syllable landed with care. "Come closer. Let me see you." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0340]] She stepped to the bedside and let the trained part of her take over, calm sliding into place with practised ease. The air carried antiseptic, sharper and cleaner than the clinic blend, threaded with something older: paper, dried herbs, a faint medicinal sweetness that did not come from a dispenser. Monitors held his vital signs in soft green light. The equipment around him was beyond anything in the salve wards, sleek, quiet, designed to anticipate failure before it happened. She recognised enough to steady her hands. The rest she filed away for later, refusing to let unfamiliarity show on her face. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0350]] Up close, the details resolved into clinical facts. His facial skin lay drawn tight, bone and hollow plainly mapped beneath it. The hair that remained was sparse and fine, lifting with the faintest current from the vents. His hands rested on the coverlet, knuckles enlarged and fingers twisted by arthritis, joints swollen into stubborn shapes. But it was his breathing that tightened something in her chest. Each inhale was shallow, edged with a faint rattle, as if his lungs had begun to forget their own rhythm and had to be reminded with effort. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0360]] "You're younger than I expected," Frost said. "They told me you were skilled, but I pictured someone older." His pale gaze did not soften. To him, she realised, salves must all read as young, brief as a season. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0370]] "I’m twenty-six, Master Frost," Marnette said. "I’ve served as a caregiver for eight years." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0380]] "Eight years." His gaze held on her face until Marnette felt flayed by it, as if he could see past skin and posture to the small, hidden calculations underneath. "That is a long time for someone so young. Do you enjoy the work?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0390]] "It’s my calling," she said, and the words came clean because they were true. "Elom gives each of us purpose. Mine is to comfort those who need comfort." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0400]] "Elom." Frost’s lips moved in something that might once have been a smile, a remembered shape rather than warmth. "So you’re faithful. Truly faithful, not merely keeping up the rituals." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0410]] "Elom designed us for our roles, Master Frost," Marnette said. "To question that is to question Elom’s wisdom." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0420]] "Would it?" He shifted, and Marnette stepped in on instinct, but he lifted a hand. "No. Don’t." The effort tightened his mouth. "I can still move, even if it costs me." He drew a breath and the rattle returned, a dry catch in his chest she could not pretend not to hear. "Tell me about Elder Maris. They said you were with her at the end." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0430]] Marnette paused, weighing each word. The dead demanded respect, and so did a Master’s attention. "Elder Maris was ready," she said. "She’d lived a full life, fifty-eight years. She was at peace with Elom’s plan." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0440]] "Fifty-eight years." Frost’s gaze slipped past the room, as if he were reading from some private page no one else could access. "A full life, you say. Did she ever wonder why? Why fifty-eight, specifically? Why not sixty, or a hundred, or five hundred?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0450]] A thin unease stirred under Marnette’s ribs. Those were not questions people asked out loud. "Elom’s timing is perfect, Master Frost," she said, keeping her tone steady. "We don’t question it." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0460]] "Of course not." He brought his gaze back to her, and the cleanness of it made her feel exposed. "You’re very good at your work, Marnette. I can see it in your hands, steady and sure. In your posture, loose but ready. In your voice, calm enough to borrow. You were made for this." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0470]] "We’re all made for our work, Master Frost," Marnette said. "That is Elom’s design." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0480]] "Is it?" He let his eyes close for a beat, and when they opened again the sharpness had receded, replaced by a tiredness that looked almost ordinary. "I’m tired now. Elara will show you to your room. We’ll talk more tomorrow." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0490]] Marnette bowed again. "Rest well, Master Frost. Elom watch over you." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0500]] As she turned to go, he spoke again. "Marnette?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0510]] "Yes, Master Frost?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0520]] "Do you ever dream of Earth?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0530]] The question wrong-footed her. Earth. The lost home, taught in lessons like a story with teeth, distant enough to feel half-imagined and still heavy with shame. The place their ancestors had left behind when leaving was no longer a choice. People did not dream of Earth. They were trained not to, as if longing itself were a kind of disobedience. Earth was failure. Earth was greed. Earth was the lesson Elom used to explain why Mars was both punishment and redemption. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0540]] "I dream of serving Elom, Master Frost," she said, and it was true enough to anchor her. "Earth is history." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0550]] "Does it?" His eyes had gone heavy, the lids lowering as if sleep were finally winning. "History has a way of becoming present. Remember that." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0560]] Elara waited in the corridor. The door sealed behind Marnette with a quiet hiss, cutting off the spill of sunlight and returning her to the corridor’s softer, regulated glow. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0570]] "He likes you," Elara said, guiding Marnette towards another door. "That’s rare. Master Frost doesn’t like many people." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0580]] "He asked about Earth," Marnette said, and immediately regretted the admission. The question felt too intimate, too strange to belong in a corridor conversation. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0590]] Elara stopped. "Did he?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0600]] "Is that unusual?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0610]] "Master Frost has spent his life studying history. The real history, not the version taught in schools." Elara resumed walking, and her pace sharpened as if the corridor had narrowed. "Listen to his questions, Marnette. But be careful with your answers." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0620]] "Careful how?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0630]] Elara stopped at a door that matched the last only in shape. This one was plain, no ornamentation, only a standard touchpad. "Some questions don’t have safe answers," she said. "Some answers don’t have safe questions." She pressed the panel and the door opened. "This is your room. The adjoining door connects to Master Frost’s chamber. He may need you in the night." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0640]] The room was small, but it was comfort on a scale she had never been given. Larger than her salve quarters, with a real bed instead of a pad, a private sanitation cubicle, and a window angled towards the agricultural domes. The luxury made her wary rather than grateful. She went to the glass and looked out at rows of green under artificial light, life organised into neat lines against the surrounding red. Someone tended those plants. Someone harvested that food. Salves, like her. Each in place, each spending their allotted years doing what the colony required. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0650]] "Your belongings have been brought from your quarters," Elara said, indicating the small bag set on the bed. "Dinner is at seventh hour. Master Frost usually eats in his room. You’ll take your meal in the small kitchen unless he summons you." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0660]] "Thank you, Elara," Marnette said. "This is more than I expected." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0670]] "Master Frost provides for those who serve him well." Elara paused at the threshold with her hand on the frame, as if she were bracing the moment. "One more thing, Marnette. This assignment isn’t like the others. When Master Frost dies, there will be a state funeral. A year of mourning. The whole colony will be required to take part." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0680]] Marnette nodded. "I know. A Master’s death is rare." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0690]] "Rare enough that people start noticing things." Elara lowered her voice further. "Be observant. Just don’t be too observant. There’s a difference." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0700]] The door sealed, and Marnette was alone. She unpacked the bag with the same deliberate care she used in the wards. Two spare tunics, her prayer book, the small carving of Elom her mother had pressed into her palm the day she left home. Familiar weight in an unfamiliar room. She set the carving on the bedside table and turned it towards the window, towards the agricultural domes’ soft glow, as if it mattered which way it watched. Towards green. Towards life. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0710]] Her stomach tightened with a low, impatient rumble. It had been hours since she’d eaten. She checked the time, sixth hour, close to dinner, and felt the pull of it like a hand. Then she pictured Frost’s shallow breaths and the silent machines beside his bed. Duty first, always. She would check on her patient before she allowed herself anything else. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0720]] The adjoining door opened without a sound. Frost’s room lay in evening mode, lights lowered to a gentle wash that left the corners in shadow. The monitors held their steady, soft signals, less a warning than a pulse made visible. He was asleep, breathing shallow but even, the rattle subdued for now. Marnette moved with quiet purpose, reading the displays, tracing lines, learning what each device watched for and how it would fail if it had to. She checked the medication timings, the emergency drawer, the supplies sealed and labelled with the precision of wealth. Everything was immaculate, maintained as if decline were an offence. Masters, she thought, did not face death the way salves did. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0730]] She was smoothing the blanket when his eyes opened. "Marnette," he whispered. "You’re still here." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0740]] "I’m assigned to you, Master Frost," Marnette said. "I’ll be here as long as you need me." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0750]] "Need me." He smiled, and this time there was no mistaking it, though the expression pulled tight across his face as if it cost him. "An interesting choice of words. Do you think I need you, Marnette? Or do you think you need me?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0760]] "I need to serve, Master Frost," Marnette said. "That’s different." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0770]] "Is it?" He tried to sit up, and Marnette went to him without thinking, easing him higher and settling the pillows to support his spine. The effort left him tight around the mouth. "Thank you," he said. "You have gentle hands. Most people are rough with me now. They think I can’t feel it." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0780]] "You can feel everything," Marnette said. "That’s what makes dying hard." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0790]] "Is that so?" He watched her again, not with the quick assessment from earlier but with a slow, almost intent attention that made her aware of every line of her own face. "You’ve watched many people die." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0800]] "It’s part of the work," Marnette said. "Elder Maris. Councilman Valen. Three others before them." The names came out like checkmarks, neat and bloodless, because letting them turn vivid would cost her sleep. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0810]] "And what did you learn from them?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0820]] Marnette took a moment, because the honest answer was never the easiest. "Everyone dies differently," she said, "but fear is always there. Even in the faithful. Even in the ones who insist they’re ready." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0830]] "Are you afraid of dying, Marnette?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0840]] The question was too personal, too close to the soft parts she kept covered. But he was a Master, and he was dying, and dying people were permitted their own rules. "I try not to think about it, Master Frost," she said. "Elom will determine my time." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0850]] "Elom." He spoke the name as if tasting it, testing whether it belonged in his mouth. "Do you ever wonder what Elom thinks about? Watching us live and die, again and again, generation after generation. Does it ever bore him?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0860]] A chill moved through Marnette that the room’s climate could not explain. "Elom doesn’t get bored, Master Frost," she said, sharper than she intended. "He’s eternal. Beyond such feelings." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0870]] "Is he?" Frost’s eyes drooped again, sleep tugging hard, but he held them open by will. "Or is that only what they tell you? What if Elom grows tired. What if he is watching, and waiting for something to change." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0880]] "Change would be chaos," Marnette said, and heard how automatic it sounded, a line placed in her mouth long before she could choose it. "The colony works because everyone accepts their role." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0890]] "The colony works." He gave a thin, dry laugh that caught in his throat. "It works because no one asks questions. Because no one remembers the first questions." His hand shook as he reached across the blanket towards the bedside table. He closed his fingers around a small device, slate-like but older, wrapped in a worn leather cover. "Take this. Read it tonight." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0900]] "Master Frost, I shouldn’t." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0910]] "You should. It’s only history, Marnette. Old history. Safe history." His fingers closed over hers as she took the device, his skin thin and dry, too delicate for the grip he insisted on. "Read it with open eyes. Not only with your mind." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0920]] The device held his warmth, heavier than she expected, as if it carried more than circuitry. "What is it?" <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0930]] "Stories. The old stories, before they were cleaned up. Before they were made safe." He released her hand and settled back into the pillows, his breath catching once before it smoothed out. "Now go. Eat your dinner. Sleep. Tomorrow will be a long day." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0940]] Marnette bowed and backed out, keeping her movements careful until the adjoining door shut behind her. Only then did she realise she was gripping the device hard enough to hurt. Her heart skittered, thoughts refusing to line up. This was not right. Masters did not pass knowledge down to salves, not anything that felt like a crack in the wall. They did not ask questions with edges. They did not speak of change, of chaos, of boredom in the divine plan, as if the plan could be examined at all. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0950]] But he was dying. Dying people said strange things. She had seen it often enough to have a name for it, the mind loosening its grip as the body failed. Elder Maris had spoken to her dead husband as if he stood at the foot of the bed. Councilman Valen had sworn he could see Earth’s blue sky through the dome. Death made the mind roam. That had to be all this was. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0960]] She ate in the small kitchen, a regulated bowl of nutrient paste and a handful of fresh greens from the domes. Her stomach accepted it, but her mouth did not. Everything tasted flat, as if the air itself had turned to dust. The device sat beside her plate, quiet and weighty, pulling at her attention with the insistence of a wound you cannot stop checking. She should not open it. It was improper. It was dangerous. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0970]] But he was a Master, and he was dying, and this was her assignment. Her duty. Her fingers slid under her collar to the prayer beads, worry-polished smooth by years of use, and she let the words form in her throat. Elom, guide me. Give me the strength to serve wisely. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0980]] After dinner she went back to her room and sat on the bed with the device resting on her lap. It woke at her touch, screen blooming into a soft glow. The interface was old, stripped down to text with no icons, no animation, none of the polished overlays she had glimpsed in Master spaces. Only lines of words, plain and unadorned, as if the thing had nothing to hide. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P0990]] The first file was titled Early Colony Records. She scrolled, expecting the usual sanctioned materials: manifests, inventories, sterile reports that turned lives into numbers. Those were there. Then the tone shifted. Personal logs. Private messages. Notes written for one set of eyes and never meant to be shared, the kind of words that did not survive the colony’s clean hands. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1000]] She read for hours, eyes burning, lids growing heavy while her mind stayed jaggedly awake. Each entry tugged at something she had been taught to keep settled. The names were familiar, the first colonists, the founder, the early councils, the beginnings of the Church. She knew the sanctioned story, the polished arc that turned chaos into purpose. These pages did not fit that shape. The details were not what she had learned. Worse, they felt real in a way the official versions never had. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1010]] According to these records, the early colonists had not been chosen by Elom at all. A human hand had picked them, the founder’s hand, sifting candidates by genetic markers and desirable traits, as if people were components to be sorted for a larger design. Whole sections were missing, corrupted, or scrubbed clean. Still, enough survived to make her stomach turn. The first plan had not matched the story she had been raised to honour, and whatever had been intended had been altered, redirected, or concealed behind new words. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1020]] Other entries spoke in clipped, clinical language about sequencing trials and lifespan extension, about pushing the body past its ordinary end and paying for it in failures no one described plainly. The pages broke apart in her hands as she read. Lines were blacked out. Paragraphs ended mid-sentence. Names and outcomes vanished behind blank space, as if someone had taken a knife to the past. What remained was worse than an answer. It was enough to pry at the seams of everything she thought she knew. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1030]] One entry, dated just before the first habitat sealed, spoke of biological castes and lifespan limitations in the tone of a design brief, not a scripture. Another set down population control through genetic programming as if it were merely logistics, a way to keep numbers compliant in a closed world. The language was cold, methodical, emptied of mercy. Marnette felt her mouth go dry as she read it, because nothing in her teachings had ever sounded like this, and yet the words sat on the screen with the blunt confidence of someone who expected to be obeyed. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1040]] The most unsettling entries came from the first years, when the Church of Elom was taking shape. Official histories framed it as a natural flowering, faith rising from need in a hard new world. These records described something else. Planning. Messaging. The careful cultivation of belief, word by word, paired with systematic control of what could be known. Marnette read until the letters blurred, because it sounded less like a church being born and more like a tool being built. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1050]] Marnette snapped the device shut and sat with it pressed to her thighs, hands shaking hard enough to sting. Dangerous. Wrong. The words did not stop simply because she stopped looking. They kept repeating, bright as warnings in the dark: selected by genetic markers, suitable for, original plan, different from implementation. Biological castes. Population control. Deliberate planning. Each phrase lodged under her skin, and she could not tell whether it was fear or clarity tightening her throat. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1060]] She knelt by the bed with her prayer beads curled in her fist and forced herself into the evening prayer, the familiar cadence she had relied on since childhood. "Elom, creator and guide, who designed us for our purpose, who gave each of us our role in the colony, who sees and knows, help me to understand your wisdom. Help me to accept your plan. Help me to serve with faith." The words kept coming, but they no longer settled inside her the way they used to. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1070]] The prayer had always anchored her, a weight in the hand and a script in the mouth that made grief bearable and duty simple. Tonight the phrases rang thin, as if they were being spoken in a room that no longer held sound. Nothing in the ritual had changed. Something in her had. Frost had placed a question in her and it sat there, small and patient, refusing to die. She knew what questions became when they were left alone in the dark. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1080]] She stood and went to the window. The agricultural domes glowed in the distance, green contained inside curves of engineered glass, life held up against a world that wanted it gone. She watched the neat rows and the steady lights and felt the colony’s paradox settle into focus. Nothing here survived by accident. It endured through design, through planning, through rules obeyed down to the smallest breath. Outside was red and indifferent. Inside was green, and made. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1090]] Or because someone had decided what should live, and how. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1100]] The thought arrived uninvited. She tried to shut it out, but it clung like the aftertaste of bitter medicine. Frost’s question came back, quiet and persistent. Did you ever dream of Earth? <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1110]] She had dreamed of Earth once, when she was small enough not to know what to silence. In the dream there was blue water without walls, green forests that did not grow in trays, rain striking her cheeks with a cold, startling tenderness. Wind moved through trees taller than any dome could hold, and the sound was not machinery, not vents, but a living hush. She had woken with her face wet, shaken by a longing for a place she had never seen. Her mother had gathered her close and rocked her, murmuring the necessary comforts. Earth was gone. Mars was home. Elom had given them this world and their purpose in it. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1120]] But what if it was not true. What if Earth was not gone, only left behind. What if the colony was not a beginning at all, but a trial run, a controlled environment with controlled people. What if their short lives were not Elom’s design, but someone else’s. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1130]] "No." The word cut through the quiet room. "That’s wrong," she told herself, voice too sharp. "That’s dangerous." <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1140]] She went back to the bed and lay down, drawing the blanket up to her shoulders as if fabric could hold the world in place. The device sat on the bedside table, screen dark, innocent in its silence. She should hand it over. She should report Frost’s questions, his insinuations, the way he had pushed at the edges of doctrine. She should. The thought stalled every time it reached her hands. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1150]] But he was dying, and she was his caregiver. Part of comfort was listening, even when the words came out crooked, even when they strayed into places she was not meant to go. Dying people reached for meaning the way a drowning person reached for air. She could tell herself this was only care, only duty, and for the moment that was enough to keep her still. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1160]] Marnette shut her eyes, but sleep stayed out of reach. The fragments kept circling behind her lids, refusing to settle: genetic markers, an original plan, a later revision. Castes. Limits. Control written as policy. Then Frost’s voice threaded through it all, quiet and impossible to forget. Do you ever dream of Earth? <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1170]] Tomorrow she would be professional. She would wear the calm mask that made others feel safe, compassionate without becoming involved, skilled without showing opinion. She would keep her thoughts leashed and her hands steady, and she would do her duty as if the night had not put new words in her mouth. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1180]] But tonight, in the dark of a room that was not hers, with Mars pressed up beyond glass and barrier, Marnette could not stop herself from wondering. The thought was small, almost harmless, and it frightened her more than any shouted heresy. Wondering led to questions. Questions led to doors that did not close again, no matter how hard you pulled. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1190]] She turned onto her side and faced the window. The agricultural domes held their steady lights, not stars but labour made visible, life kept alive by schedules and hands. Beyond them lay the red expanse and the hard dark, and beyond that, whatever truth Frost had put within reach. The pull of it tightened in her chest. Answers might exist. Wanting them was the first danger. Seeking them would be the second, and she did not have the right to either. <!-- REV2 -->

[[CH01-P1200]] Sleep came in fits, not a clean surrender so much as her body giving up in stages. In the last clear moment before she slipped under, Marnette knew this assignment had already stepped outside the boundaries of the others. She had arrived certain of her place, certain of the shape of the world. Now a handful of words had lodged inside her and would not dissolve. She could still rise tomorrow, wash, dress, perform competence with steady hands and a steady voice. She could still say the prayers. But she would be saying them with something new in the room with her, a thin, sharp awareness she could not put back where it belonged. Whatever Frost intended, whatever the records contained, the person who had boarded the transport that morning was already gone. <!-- REV2 -->
